essential_question: Why must two planes meet in a line — never just a point?
objective: Justify why two intersecting planes meet in a line (not a point) and two intersecting lines meet in a point, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on planes, sets, union, intersection, and subset.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a plane and name it two ways. Then write the four set symbols (∪, ∩, ⊂, ∅) from memory and say what each one means.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 2A & 2B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 2A/2B. We check the tricky ones: naming a plane by three points vs. one letter, whether a set can be a subset of itself, and reading union/intersection notation. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: two distinct lines that cross meet in exactly one point. Argue it — what would it mean if they shared two points instead?
Claim 2: two distinct planes that meet always meet in a line, never just a point. Argue it — think of two walls, or two flat sheets pushed together.
Claim 3: a plane contains infinitely many points. Argue it — same idea as a line, one dimension up.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 3 — same rhythm: Concept, Build, Prove.

success_criteria.text: I can symbolize and identify planes, sets, union, intersection, and subset without help
I can justify why two planes intersect in a line and two lines intersect in a point
I can explain what makes a plane 'flat' (two-dimensional) and why it holds infinitely many points

questions.explain: A plane is two-dimensional — it has length and width but no thickness — and it extends infinitely in every direction.

questions.prompt: The intersection of two distinct planes is always a…
questions.explain: When two flat planes meet (and aren't the same plane), they share exactly the points along one straight line — think of two walls meeting at the corner seam.

questions.prompt: The intersection of two distinct, non-parallel lines is always a…
questions.explain: Two straight lines that aren't parallel cross at exactly one shared location.

questions.prompt: Let A = {red, blue, green} and B = {blue, green, yellow}. Write A ∪ B and A ∩ B.
questions.answer: A ∪ B = {red, blue, green, yellow}; A ∩ B = {blue, green}.
questions.explain: Union combines every element from both sets without repeating; intersection keeps only what both sets share (blue and green).

questions.prompt: Draw two overlapping circles for Set A and Set B. Shade only the region that represents A ∩ B.
questions.answer: Two overlapping circles labeled A and B, with only the lens-shaped overlap shaded.
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly overlapping circles labeled A and B (1 pt) + only the shared overlap region shaded, not the crescents (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why two planes can never meet at just a single point.
questions.answer: A plane is flat and extends infinitely in every direction; if two flat, non-parallel planes touched at even one point, they would have to keep spreading apart from each other all around that point — but because both are perfectly flat, the points where they agree line up straight, forming a whole line, not just one dot.
questions.explain: Full credit: names flatness/infinite extent as the reason the shared points must line up straight (a line) instead of stopping at one point.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 2 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
